title: oVirt / RHV Storage Pool Manager (SPM) Lease Contention, Loss, and Transition Risk
exclusions: In scope: oVirt 4.4+ or Red Hat Virtualization 4.4+ data centers with shared block, file, or Gluster storage where the SPM is the elected single-host coordinator for storage metadata and ovirt:engine plus per-host ovirt:vdsm logs are reachable to a Universal Forwarder or Heavy Forwarder, optionally accompanied by ovirt:audit and ovirt:sanlock so engine narrative, hypervisor lock activity, and lease state can be co-correlated in one rollup. Excludes UC-19.1.41 (generic ovirt:engine ERROR or SEVERE burst with no SPM-specific lease, IrsBroker, or VDSM lock context — that UC pages on Java logger volume, this UC pages on storage coordinator narrative). Excludes UC-19.1.43 (VDSM management reconnect or vds:Client agent session churn against the engine plane; if vdsm.log is dominated by disconnect or reconnect lines without IrsBroker, lockSpace, getSpmStatus, or sanlock keywords, route the case to 19.1.43, not here). Excludes standalone libvirt or KVM hosts without an oVirt Engine — without an SPM election there is no coordinator state to lose. Excludes Oracle Linux Virtualization Manager (OLVM) running as an Oracle Cloud Infrastructure managed service, where file paths, telemetry, and support contracts diverge enough that this UC does not reliably ship — open a parallel UC against the OCI vendor surface. Excludes Ceph or storage-array-only signals as the sole indicator: a noisy SAN does not, by itself, prove SPM contention; correlate this UC with array, multipath, and HBA UCs only after engine and VDSM both confirm the storage-pool semantic. Excludes Proxmox VE, Nutanix AHV, VMware vSAN, and OpenStack Cinder stacks — their coordinator concepts (HA agent, Cassandra metadata, vSAN owner, Cinder scheduler) are different in mechanism and operator workflow and live in their own cat-19 UCs.
dataSources: `index=virtualization` with `sourcetype=ovirt:engine` (Java multiline `engine.log`: `IrsBrokerCommand`, `IrsManager`, `SPM_ACQUIRE_FAILED`, `SPM_RELEASE_FAILED`, “Failed to acquire SPM lease”, “Spm Status: Free” persistence, `ClusterCommandBase` / SPM watchdog language); `sourcetype=ovirt:vdsm` (multiline `vdsm.log` from `monitor:///var/log/vdsm/vdsm.log` on every hypervisor: `getSpmStatus`, `lockSpace`, `connectStoragePool`, sanlock/lease phrasing); `sourcetype=ovirt:audit` (engine_audit SPM transitions); optional `sourcetype=ovirt:sanlock` or `syslog`+props for `/var/log/sanlock.log`; optional `linux:journald`. Named inputs: UF `monitor://` for engine.log, engine_audit.log, and per-host vdsm.log; optional SC4S for relayed sanlock. Do not use fake sourcetypes (e.g. ovirt:spm) that return no events.
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder (or Heavy Forwarder) with `monitor://` to /var/log/ovirt-engine/engine.log, /var/log/ovirt-engine/engine_audit.log, and /var/log/vdsm/vdsm.log, each to index=virtualization with props.conf from this use case. No first-party oVirt/RHV Splunkbase add-on is required: ship the raw files with custom props, as in UC-19.1.41 for the engine, extended here to VDSM on every host.
spl:
index=virtualization (sourcetype="ovirt:engine" OR sourcetype="ovirt:vdsm" OR sourcetype="ovirt:audit") earliest=-1h
| eval src_kind=case(sourcetype="ovirt:engine","engine", sourcetype="ovirt:vdsm","vdsm", sourcetype="ovirt:audit","audit", 1=1,"other")
| eval log_lvl=upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(log_level, level, severity, loglevel, logLevel, ""))))
| eval is_eng_spm_fail=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(SPM_ACQUIRE_FAILED|SPM_RELEASE_FAILED|Failed to acquire\s+SPM\s+lease|SPM\s*lease.*expir)"),1,0)
| eval is_eng_spm_stuck_free=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)Spm Status:\s*Free"),1,0)
| eval is_eng_watchdog=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(IrsManager|IrsBrokerCommand|ClusterCommandBase|SPM).{0,160}(?i)(watchdog|timed? out|time[- ]?out)"),1,0)
| eval is_vdsm_spm=if(src_kind="vdsm" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(getSpmStatus|lockSpace|connectStoragePool)"),1,0)
| eval is_vdsm_lock_err=if(is_vdsm_spm=1 AND match(_raw, "(?i)(fail|error|timeout|cannot|unable|No\s+space|errno)"),1,0)
| eval is_sanlock=if(match(_raw, "(?i)sanlock.+(?i)(add_lockspace|acquire|host_id|delta|lock|lease)"),1,0)
| eval is_split_hint=if(match(_raw, "(?i)SPM.+(?i)(concurrent|multiple|contend|split|acquire)"),1,0)
| eval is_sd_spm=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(StorageDomain|storage domain).{0,120}(?i)(SPM|spm|disconnect)"),1,0)
| eval _evlen=len(_raw)
| bin _time span=5m aligntime=earliest
| stats sum(is_eng_spm_fail) as n_eng_spm_fail, sum(is_eng_spm_stuck_free) as n_spm_free_prints, sum(is_eng_watchdog) as n_spm_watchdog, sum(is_vdsm_lock_err) as n_vdsm_lock_err, sum(is_sanlock) as n_sanlock, sum(is_split_hint) as n_split_hint, sum(is_sd_spm) as n_sd_spm, dc(host) as host_dc, max(_evlen) as maxraw by _time
| eval has_cross=if(n_eng_spm_fail>0 AND n_vdsm_lock_err>0,1,0)
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    has_cross=1 OR n_eng_spm_fail>=3 OR n_vdsm_lock_err>=5 OR n_spm_watchdog>=2 OR n_sanlock>=4,
    "critical",
    n_eng_spm_fail>0 OR n_vdsm_lock_err>0 OR n_sd_spm>0 OR n_sanlock>=2 OR n_spm_free_prints>=4 OR n_split_hint>=1,
    "warning",
    n_spm_free_prints>0 OR n_spm_watchdog>0 OR n_split_hint>0,
    "watch",
    1=1, "ok")
| sort 0 - _time
| table _time, n_eng_spm_fail, n_vdsm_lock_err, n_spm_free_prints, n_spm_watchdog, n_sanlock, n_split_hint, n_sd_spm, has_cross, severity_bucket, host_dc, maxraw
description: Detects oVirt or RHV Storage Pool Manager (SPM) failure modes from co-ingested `ovirt:engine` (Irs/SPM acquire/release, lease expiry, Spm Status: Free, watchdog/timeouts) plus `ovirt:vdsm` and optional `ovirt:audit` for contention and state transitions. Five-minute rollups with `coalesce` on log level fields, `src_kind` for engine vs vdsm, `has_cross` when engine failure patterns align with VDSM lock/SPM line errors, and a four-tier `severity_bucket` (critical / warning / watch / ok). This is not UC-19.1.41 (generic Java ERROR/SEVERE burst in engine.log only) and not UC-19.1.43 (VDSM reconnect / agent session churn to the management channel). It does not cover standalone KVM without oVirt SPM, or Oracle OLVM in OCI (different operations plane).
value: SPM is the single coordinator for shared-storage metadata: without a valid holder or with repeated contention, live merge for backups, template copies, storage-domain attach, DR tests, and live storage migration can stall with tasks stuck in oVirt. The blast radius is “storage-wide workflow stops,” not a single misbehaving Java logger. Catching this early routes incidents to the virtualization/storage boundary before business windows (backup, maintenance, DR) are missed, and it separates the story from all-purpose engine error rates and from host-agent reconnection patterns.
implementation: Deploy Universal Forwarder monitors and props in Step 1, confirm five sample events per sourcetype in Step 3, then schedule the `spl` every five minutes. Alert on severity_bucket warning or critical with suppression on incident bucket. Reconcile with Administration Portal Events when triaging; keep UC-19.1.41 and 19.1.43 runbooks separate per exclusions.
visualization: Timechart: 5m bin overlay of n_eng_spm_fail vs n_vdsm_lock_err, markers for has_cross=1. Single value: worst severity_bucket in 60m. Timechart: stacked severity by bin. Table: n_eng_spm_fail, n_vdsm_lock_err, n_spm_free_prints, n_spm_watchdog, n_sanlock, n_split_hint, has_cross, host_dc, maxraw. Bar chart: host weight when n_vdsm_lock_err>0.

references.title: Red Hat Virtualization 4.4 — Administration guide (managing storage)
Red Hat — Collecting logs (engine and host context)

knownFalsePositives: (1) Planned SPM handoff during a documented maintenance window where Events shows the same user or job moving SPM by design; pair with your change calendar before paging. (2) Lab STONITH or fencing exercise that prints one acquire string then clears in Events—tune n_* floors in production. (3) Rolling host upgrade with RHV-documented SPM order; expect a short burst, not a sustained multi-hour pattern. (4) Storage array microcode pause: VDSM may show lock-like verbs while paths return; correlate with array and multipath UCs, not this alert alone. (5) Intentional engine-cleanup or lockspace reset after a rehearsed split scenario in UAT—use a separate index or suppress by environment. (6) Post-upgrade SPM re-election noise with no stuck user tasks and quiet Events—downgrade to watch. (7) Multi-DC DR drill where SPM is bounced on purpose; load a suppression lookup for that change id. (8) Storage domain reattach: brief sanlock re-acquire, usually one 5m bin, not a rising trend. (9) Backup software snapshot quiesce causing transient VDSM cannot lines without an engine SPM failure—pair with 19.1.41 and backup UCs. (10) Synthetic load on APIs that do not touch storage coordination—Events should show no storage task backlog. (11) Greenfield first install: no SPM elected yet; Events may still read configuring—hold alerts for first 24h or a static cluster-age lookup. (12) iSCSI path flap during cable maintenance—VDSM errors without Irs/SPM strings; route to network/SAN runbooks if engine stays clean.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• oVirt Engine 4.4+ / RHV 4.4+ (or 4.5+ oVirt) with shared block or file storage; **Storage Pool Manager (SPM)** is the one-host coordinator the engine elects for storage metadata changes. Product UI labels (data center, storage pool) vary, but the log signatures below are grep-friendly.
• **Universal Forwarder** on the Engine for engine.log and engine_audit.log, and on every oVirt hypervisor for vdsm.log. Without per-host vdsm.log you cannot run this use case: UC-19.1.41 is engine-only; this one is not.
• NTP or chrony everywhere: sanlock and SPM watchdog behavior are timing-sensitive. Skewed hosts produce timeout-looking lines with no true fault.
• Splunk index (example virtualization) and a search role with rights to that index. Plan retention long enough to compare a slow SPM transition across a full backup window (often 7–30 days for ops review).

Index sizing (order of magnitude only): vdsm is chatty. Budget roughly 1–3 KB per line at peak, multiple lines per host per minute during storage tasks, times host count. Engine logs are smaller but can spike during upgrades. A three-host lab is tiny; a forty-host block-iSCSI farm needs license headroom before you add optional sanlock and journald feeds. Start with the three required sourcetypes, then add optional ovirt:sanlock and linux:journald only if a ticket proves you need the extra detail, so you do not double-count the same line.

Deployment Server pattern: ship one TA bundle (for example TA-ovirt-virtualization) to an engine class and a second serverclass to all hypervisor hosts. The only difference between bundles is which inputs.conf stanzas are enabled. Keep crcSalt = <SOURCE> on each monitor to survive logrotate inode changes. Use $decideOnStartup for host unless you override with the cluster FQDN for clarity in multisite.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**

On the Engine, install or extend your forwarder app with the following stanzas (paths are the defaults for RHEL-derivative oVirt Engine installs; adjust if your org relocates var):

```ini
[monitor:///var/log/ovirt-engine/engine.log]
disabled = 0
host = $decideOnStartup
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
index = virtualization
sourcetype = ovirt:engine

[monitor:///var/log/ovirt-engine/engine_audit.log]
disabled = 0
host = $decideOnStartup
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
index = virtualization
sourcetype = ovirt:audit
```

On every hypervisor:

```ini
[monitor:///var/log/vdsm/vdsm.log]
disabled = 0
host = $decideOnStartup
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
index = virtualization
sourcetype = ovirt:vdsm
```

Optional sanlock (confirm path: ls -l /var/log/sanlock* on a host with block storage):

```ini
[monitor:///var/log/sanlock.log]
disabled = 0
index = virtualization
sourcetype = ovirt:sanlock
```

props.conf for ovirt:engine — match UC-19.1.41 so stack traces stay with the Java line that opened the event; the **LINE_BREAKER** is anchored to a line that begins with the engine timestamp, not a continuation.

```ini
[ovirt:engine]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}\s\d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}
TRUNCATE = 10000
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 23
TIME_PREFIX = ^
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S,%3N
EXTRACT-engine_line = ^(?<log_level>\S+)\s+\[(?<logger>[^\]]+)\]\s*\((?<thread>[^)]*)\)\s*\[(?<correlation_id>[A-Fa-f0-9-]{0,40})\]\s*(?<message>.*)$
```

props.conf for ovirt:vdsm — VDSM often prints a priority token, thread name, and Python tracebacks. Keep one logical failure inside one event by breaking on a new timestamp. Sample two or three real files in Step 3 before you freeze **TIME_FORMAT**; some builds add timezone offsets. If a line does not start with a digit, your **LINE_BREAKER** must still fire on the pattern vdsm actually uses (some sites log with a short hostname prefix). Document the one-line prefix you used in a comment above the stanza in props.conf for the next admin.

```ini
[ovirt:vdsm]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)(?:\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}|\S+\s+\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})
TRUNCATE = 10000
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
TIME_PREFIX = ^
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S,%3N
```

ovirt:audit is usually single line:

```ini
[ovirt:audit]
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
TRUNCATE = 20000
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S
TIME_PREFIX = ^
```

After deployment: on each class of host run sudo tail on the file, then in Splunk search the last hour and confirm _time drifts at most seconds from wall clock. If _time is wrong, fix **TIME_FORMAT** and TZ= on the forwarder before you tune alerts.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**

The spl field in this JSON is the canonical copy. In Splunk, open **Settings → Searches, reports, and alerts** → New Report → run over All time (or -1h) to verify, then save as an alert with schedule */5, earliest -65m@m, latest now, and trigger on results where severity_bucket is in (warning,critical).

```spl
index=virtualization (sourcetype="ovirt:engine" OR sourcetype="ovirt:vdsm" OR sourcetype="ovirt:audit") earliest=-1h
| eval src_kind=case(sourcetype="ovirt:engine","engine", sourcetype="ovirt:vdsm","vdsm", sourcetype="ovirt:audit","audit", 1=1,"other")
| eval log_lvl=upper(trim(tostring(coalesce(log_level, level, severity, loglevel, logLevel, ""))))
| eval is_eng_spm_fail=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(SPM_ACQUIRE_FAILED|SPM_RELEASE_FAILED|Failed to acquire\s+SPM\s+lease|SPM\s*lease.*expir)"),1,0)
| eval is_eng_spm_stuck_free=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)Spm Status:\s*Free"),1,0)
| eval is_eng_watchdog=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(IrsManager|IrsBrokerCommand|ClusterCommandBase|SPM).{0,160}(?i)(watchdog|timed? out|time[- ]?out)"),1,0)
| eval is_vdsm_spm=if(src_kind="vdsm" AND match(_raw, "(?i)(getSpmStatus|lockSpace|connectStoragePool)"),1,0)
| eval is_vdsm_lock_err=if(is_vdsm_spm=1 AND match(_raw, "(?i)(fail|error|timeout|cannot|unable|No\s+space|errno)"),1,0)
| eval is_sanlock=if(match(_raw, "(?i)sanlock.+(?i)(add_lockspace|acquire|host_id|delta|lock|lease)"),1,0)
| eval is_split_hint=if(match(_raw, "(?i)SPM.+(?i)(concurrent|multiple|contend|split|acquire)"),1,0)
| eval is_sd_spm=if(src_kind IN ("engine","audit") AND match(_raw, "(?i)(StorageDomain|storage domain).{0,120}(?i)(SPM|spm|disconnect)"),1,0)
| eval _evlen=len(_raw)
| bin _time span=5m aligntime=earliest
| stats sum(is_eng_spm_fail) as n_eng_spm_fail, sum(is_eng_spm_stuck_free) as n_spm_free_prints, sum(is_eng_watchdog) as n_spm_watchdog, sum(is_vdsm_lock_err) as n_vdsm_lock_err, sum(is_sanlock) as n_sanlock, sum(is_split_hint) as n_split_hint, sum(is_sd_spm) as n_sd_spm, dc(host) as host_dc, max(_evlen) as maxraw by _time
| eval has_cross=if(n_eng_spm_fail>0 AND n_vdsm_lock_err>0,1,0)
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    has_cross=1 OR n_eng_spm_fail>=3 OR n_vdsm_lock_err>=5 OR n_spm_watchdog>=2 OR n_sanlock>=4,
    "critical",
    n_eng_spm_fail>0 OR n_vdsm_lock_err>0 OR n_sd_spm>0 OR n_sanlock>=2 OR n_spm_free_prints>=4 OR n_split_hint>=1,
    "warning",
    n_spm_free_prints>0 OR n_spm_watchdog>0 OR n_split_hint>0,
    "watch",
    1=1, "ok")
| sort 0 - _time
| table _time, n_eng_spm_fail, n_vdsm_lock_err, n_spm_free_prints, n_spm_watchdog, n_sanlock, n_split_hint, n_sd_spm, has_cross, severity_bucket, host_dc, maxraw
```

The SPL above is the exact copy from the `spl` field; keep them in sync if you edit either. The eval chain keeps **coalesce**-ready field names; the detector keys off _raw regexes so a half-built TA does not zero out the runbook.

For webhook and ticket bodies include: _time, severity_bucket, has_cross, the five-minute counts, and host dc (how many forwarders reported in-bin). Throttle: suppress 15 to 30 minutes on the rounded _time bucket to avoid the same storm paging every five minutes. Do not route watch to PagerDuty in week one; validate noise first.

**Step 3 — Validate (and differentiate from siblings)**

(a) Sample five events: index=virtualization (sourcetype=ovirt:engine OR ovirt:vdsm OR ovirt:audit) | head 5 per sourcetype in separate tabs. You must see real host fields and plausible _raw, not HEC test payloads.

(b) Engine grep: on the engine VM sudo grep -iE 'SPM|IrsBroker|IrsManager' on engine.log; confirm Splunk returns the same class of lines in-window.

(c) VDSM grep: on a host sudo grep -iE 'getSpmStatus|lockSpace|connectStoragePool' on vdsm.log; confirm the forwarder for that host is the same FQDN you see in Splunk's host.

(d) Sibling: UC-19.1.41 fires on high ERROR/SEVERE mass from ovirt:engine only; it will page for JDBC, directory, and REST noise that never mentions SPM. This UC (19.1.42) must include ovirt:vdsm in the base OR. If you delete vdsm from the search, you re-built the wrong problem.

(e) Sibling: UC-19.1.43 (VDSM reconnect / agent session churn) measures disconnect storm patterns to the management plane, not SPM lease acquisition. If _raw is dominated by vds::Client disconnect lines without Irs/SPM text, that ticket belongs to 19.1.43, not here.

(f) Out of scope: standalone libvirt or KVM without oVirt Engine; Oracle Linux Virtualization Manager cloud service (different file layout and support path) — do not expect this SPL to be your only signal there.

(g) In **Administration Portal → Events**, open the Events list for the same interval as a warning bin. You want narrative match (SPM start/stop, storage domain) even if the UI uses different words than the log.

**Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboards and handoff)**

Build a small dashboard: first row, single value for worst severity_bucket in the last 60m and a count of bins in critical. Second row, timechart of n_eng_spm_fail and n_vdsm_lock_err overlaid, 5m span, so cross-host correlation (has_cross) is visible. Third row, table of the last 24 bins with n_spm_free_prints, n_spm_watchdog, n_sanlock, and n_split_hint to tell “nobody won SPM” from “sanlock is noisy.” Fourth row, optional: bar of host by count when n_vdsm_lock_err>0 to see spread.

Runbook handoff: the virtualization platform team owns the SPM election story, storage team owns array and multipath, network owns MTU and iSCSI drops. Cite this UC in the handoff, not a generic 'engine errors' Jira template. When has_cross=1, open both engine and vdsm in parallel: do not triage in isolation.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting (nine oVirt-SPM failure modes)**

(1) Sanlock lease or lockspace name drift after a major upgrade: engine and host logs disagree on identifiers; the alert may show sanlock hits without a clean story. Use vendor KCS to validate lockspace cleanup; extend regex only after reading five real sanlock lines from your farm.

(2) LINE_BREAKER or SHOULD_LINEMERGE mis-set for ovirt:vdsm: getSpmStatus and lockSpace appear split across two Splunk events and the _raw match misses. Re-open props.conf with a real failing sample and anchor the breaker to the first character of a new vdsm line in your build.

(3) Host clock skew: Irs/SPM watchdog timeouts in logs with healthy storage. Fix chrony, then re-run the search.

(4) oVirt 4.5+ message reword: Java class or substring moves; keep relying on _raw and periodically diff a lab upgrade before production minor bumps.

(5) Large farm, sparse coincidence: has_cross=1 with n_eng_spm_fail=1 and n_vdsm_lock_err=1 might be chance alignment; tighten the alert child search with a second threshold on one side if ops proves noise.

(6) engine_audit not collected: ovirt:audit is empty; you lose CSV-style state transitions. Enable the second monitor on the engine.

(7) Typo in sourcetype name: metadata shows ovirt:spm from a test — that name is not valid for this runbook. Only ovirt:engine, ovirt:vdsm, ovirt:audit, optional ovirt:sanlock per prerequisites.

(8) Hypervisor UF missing: only engine hosts in results; vdsm lock counts never move. The use case is not implementable without every host’s vdsm.log.

(9) Double ingest: you enabled linux:journald and UF monitor of the same vdsm line — counts double. Pick one path per host.

SC4S optional path: if you already aggregate host syslog to a central SC4S tier, you can route sanlock or kernel messages that mention lease_contend into the same index with a dedicated sourcetype or a transforms-based override. Do not rely on syslog alone for engine or vdsm files: file monitor gives you complete multiline fidelity. If you must use syslog for sanlock, tune LINE_BREAKER on the receiving Heavy Forwarder identically to what you would have used on the host, and accept that you may lose partial lines under burst.

Heavy Forwarder note: some sites forbid UFs on hypervisors and use a subnet-scoped HF with NFS or SSH file collection. That is supported as long as the same props.conf ships to indexers and search heads and the effective sourcetype names stay ovirt:vdsm. Latency will be higher; widen the alert window to 15m if network collection adds more than two minutes of lag.

Search-time field hygiene: the SPL in this use case does not require successful extraction of log_level. If you add props EXTRACTs later, the coalesce-ready eval still helps dashboards that use those fields, but the alert logic is intentionally _raw-driven so a parser regression does not silence SPM detection.

Multisite: one Splunk can hold multiple clusters. If you must separate blast radius, set host naming or add an index or source override per data center, then clone the search with a prefix constraint. The stats layer here does not use cluster name from the API because this UC is log-only; if you have a lookup from host to cluster, join it after the stats for cleaner titles.

Sample healthy versus unhealthy bin: a healthy five-minute window often shows zero n_eng_spm_fail and zero n_vdsm_lock_err even under VM churn. An unhealthy window typically shows n_eng_spm_fail>=1, or n_vdsm_lock_err growing on multiple hosts, or n_spm_free_prints rising without a matching Storage Domain activation in Events in Events. A cross signal is has_cross=1, which the triage should treat as higher urgency than a lone vdsm line.

Post-incident: export the five-minute table for the incident window, attach the vendor ticket and Events screenshots, and file a follow-up to reduce duplicate ingest if you discover journald+monitor double counting.

RHV versus oVirt: message strings in engine.log are broadly aligned but not identical. Do not add brittle exact string equality in prod; the regexes already favor token families (SPM_ACQUIRE_FAILED, lockSpace) over full sentences. After any RHV maintenance pack, re-run Step 3(a) for five samples.

Read-only oVirt UI role: you do not need admin API to validate this use case, only the Events view and the ability to read logs. That keeps separation of duties clean for Splunk teams that do not get Engine admin accounts.

HEC: not required. If you later add HEC for lab automation, set sourcetype in the HEC event metadata to ovirt:engine or ovirt:vdsm to avoid creating yet another ad hoc name.
grandmaExplanation: We picture shared oVirt and RHV storage like a seaport with one qualified harbor pilot who must clear every large ship before it moves. We watch for that pilot being ashore, two pilots fighting over the same slot, or the radio going quiet mid-move. We help your team act before long disk tasks and weekend backups are stuck for hours for lack of a safe coordinator.
hardware: Typical self-managed oVirt or RHV: RHEL 8 or 9 engine VM with four vCPU and sixteen to twenty-four GB RAM; hypervisors on Dell PowerEdge or HPE ProLiant Gen10+ bare metal with Intel Xeon Scalable or AMD EPYC, dual 25 GbE uplinks, and shared storage over Fibre Channel, iSCSI, or NFSv4.1. File domains on Gluster still use the same SPM coordinator. NTP on all nodes is mandatory.
